coredata.dc:title: DNA nanosensor based on biocompatible graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Qian, Zhao Sheng
Shan, Xiao Yue
Chai, Lu Jing
Ma, Juan Juan
Chen, Jian Rong
Feng, Hui
coredata.dc:description: An ultrasensitive nanosensor based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between biocompatible graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes for DNA detection was reported. We take advantage of good biocompatibility and strong fluorescence of graphene quantum dots, base pairing specificity of DNA and unique fluorescence resonance energy transfer between graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes to achieve the analysis of low concentrations of DNA. Graphene quantum dots with high quantum yield up to 0.20 were prepared and served as the fluorophore of DNA probe. FRET process between graphene quantum dots-labeled probe and oxidized carbon nanotubes is easily achieved due to their efficient self-assembly through specific π–π interaction. This nanosensor can distinguish complementary and mismatched nucleic acid sequences with high sensitivity and good reproducibility. The detection method based on this nanosensor possesses a broad linear span of up to 133.0nM and ultralow detection limit of 0.4nM. The constructed nanosensor is expected to be highly biocompatible because of all its components with excellent biocompatibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Carbon nanotubes
Nanosensor
DNA detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs)
Principle of graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes based nanosensor for DNA detection
Design and construction of graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes based nanosensor for DNA detection
Detection of DNA with graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes based nanosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of oxidized carbon nanotubes (A), ssDNA–rGQDs probe (B), and mixture between oxidized carbon nanotubes and ssDNA–rGQDs probe (C and D).

The fluorescence spectra (A) and time-resolved decay curves (B) of GQDs, rGQDs, cDNA, tDNA, ssDNA–rGQDs and dsDNA–rGQDs. Inset: fluorescence images of GQDs, rGQDs and ssDNA–rGQDs. Relative fluorescence intensity represents fluorescence intensities relative to background.

The quenching effect of CNTs on the fluorescence of ssDNA–rGQDs and following fluorescence recovery by tDNA and mDNA (A), and UV–vis spectra of ssDNA–rGQDs, CNTs and ssDNA–rGQDs/CNTs (B). (a) ssDNA–rGQDs, (b) ssDNA–rGQDs+tDNA (107.0nM), (c) ssDNA–rGQDs+CNTs (3.2μgmL−1), (d) ssDNA–rGQDs+CNTs (3.2μgmL−1)+tDNA (107.0nM), (e) ssDNA–rGQDs+CNTs (3.2μgmL−1)+mDNA-1 (107.0nM), (f) ssDNA–rGQDs+CNTs (3.2μgmL−1)+mDNA-2 (107.0nM) and (g) ssDNA–rGQDs+CNTs (3.2μgmL−1)+mDNA-3 (107.0nM).

(A) The fluorescence quenching of ssDNA-rGQDs with various concentrations of CNTs. (B) Time dependence of the fluorescence quenching with 3.2µgmL−1 of CNTs and the fluorescence recovery with 133.0nM of tDNA.

(A) The fluorescence recovery of ssDNA–rCQDs/CNTs system after incubation with various concentrations of tDNA. (B) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and concentration of tDNA.
Schematic illustration of DNA detection strategy of nanosensor based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes.

head.ce:title: DNA nanosensor based on biocompatible graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An ultrasensitive nanosensor based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between biocompatible graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes for DNA detection was reported. We take advantage of good biocompatibility and strong fluorescence of graphene quantum dots, base pairing specificity of DNA and unique fluorescence resonance energy transfer between graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes to achieve the analysis of low concentrations of DNA. Graphene quantum dots with high quantum yield up to 0.20 were prepared and served as the fluorophore of DNA probe. FRET process between graphene quantum dots-labeled probe and oxidized carbon nanotubes is easily achieved due to their efficient self-assembly through specific π–π interaction. This nanosensor can distinguish complementary and mismatched nucleic acid sequences with high sensitivity and good reproducibility. The detection method based on this nanosensor possesses a broad linear span of up to 133.0nM and ultralow detection limit of 0.4nM. The constructed nanosensor is expected to be highly biocompatible because of all its components with excellent biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomolecular detection has shown more and more critical application in early diagnostics and treatment of diseases as well as industrial and environmental monitoring, which has prompted intense interest in the development of rapid, trustworthy, simple and low-cost biosensors for nucleic acids, proteins and other wide range of biomolecules (Giljohann and Mirkin, 2009; Etzioni et al., 2003). In the past decade, nanotechnology has been playing an increasingly important role in the improvement of biosensing (Swierczewska et al., 2012; Mayer and Hafner, 2011). Due to their quantum effects stemming from the large surface area-to-volume ratio, nanomaterials including Au nanoparticles, semiconductor QDs and graphene quantum dots possess unique optical, electrical, catalytic and magnetic properties compared to their bulk structures, and thus are widely applied to the detection of biological species (Chen et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2011; Han et al., 2001).

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) as a sensitive and reliable analytic strategy is widely used in biological analysis (Jares-Erijman and Jovin, 2003). Excellent donor–acceptor pairs are extremely critical to improve the efficiency of fluorescence resonance energy transfer and the resulting analytical performance. Good biocompatibility of donor–acceptor pairs is another significant factor for practical application of constructed sensors in vivo. However, current-used fluorescent probes including organic dyes and inorganic semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) have their own drawbacks. Poor photostability, easy photobleaching, small Stokes shifts and short lifetimes shown by organic dyes push scientists to use QDs as the alternative choice since semiconductor QDs overcome these drawbacks. But their high toxicity, common blinking behavior and relatively high cost still do not enable them as an outstanding probe (Wu et al., 2003; Howarth et al., 2008; Michalet et al., 2005; Somers et al., 2007). Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have shown excellent performance in photovoltaic devices, catalysis and biological imaging since their recent discovery (Gupta et al., 2011; Yan et al., 2010; Li et al., 2010). Compared with conventional dye molecules and semiconductor QDs, GQDs possess great advantages including low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, good resistance to photobleaching, stable emission and easy modulation (Cao et al., 2007; Yang et al., 2009). In view of points of fluorescent probe, GQDs are expected as a promising biocompatible material in bioimaging and biodetection due to its excellent fluorescence performance (Lin et al., 2011; C. Zhao et al., 2011; H.X. Zhao et al., 2011; J. Zhao et al., 2011; Shi et al., 2012). Therefore, GQDs were chosen as the outstanding probe instead of organic dyes and semiconductor QDs for construction of DNA sensor in this work.

Due to their unique one-dimensional structure, distinct electrical and spectroscopic properties, rich chemical and mechanical properties, carbon nanotubes have been extensively employed for biosensing applications (Wong et al., 1998; Zhang et al., 2010; Kim et al., 2009; C. Zhao et al., 2011; H.X. Zhao et al., 2011; J. Zhao et al., 2011). Taking advantage of the noncovalent interaction of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and DNA, many fluorescence sensing platforms for dye-labeled biological molecule detection were built based on carbon nanotubes (Yang et al., 2008a,b; Lu et al., 2009; He et al., 2010). It is found that CNTs can be used to quench fluorescence of organic dyes, which shows carbon nanotubes is a good electron acceptor in FRET. The strong π–π interaction between GQDs and CNTs can be easily formed because GQDs and CNTs have the similar conjugated structures, which enables them as excellent donor–acceptor pair in FRET. Specific strong π–π interaction between GQDs and CNTs as well as the known adsorption between DNA and CNTs are expected to make CNTs as an excellent quenching agent with higher quenching efficiency (Yang et al., 2008a,b; Zhang et al., 2013). Moreover, it is reported that CNTs can be biodegraded under the catalysis of human neutrophile enzyme myeloperoxidase, which implies their good biocompatibility and thus provides the opportunity of CNTs-based DNA sensor in the potential application in vivo (Kagan et al., 2010).

In this article, a high selective and sensitive fluorescent nanosensor for DNA detection based on biocompatible graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes was constructed. Good biocompatibility and strong fluorescence of GQDs, base pairing specificity of DNA and unique fluorescence resonance energy transfer between GQDs and CNTs were taken advantage of to achieve quantitative analysis of DNA. The biocompatible graphene quantum dots possess high fluorescence quantum yield up to 0.21 after reduction with NaBH4, serving as the fluorophore of highly sensitive probe. Oxidized carbon nanotubes were prepared and performed as an effective quenching agent. Specific fluorescence “on-off-on” process originated from fluorescence quenching owing to FRET between graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes and subsequent fluorescence recovery due to liberation of free double-stranded DNA probe was employed for the first time to achieve quantitative analysis of target DNA.

Briefly, 1.0g of MWNTs were oxidized with 100mL of HNO3/H2SO4 (1:3, v/v) in an ultrasonic bath for 24h. After repetitive washing with water to neutral pH, the resulted CNTs were dried in the vacuum drying oven at 60°C for 24h. 17.5mg of CNTs were dispersed in 10mL of water, and the final concentration of CNTs was 1.75mgmL−1.

Graphite power (0.3g) was added into a mixture of concentrated H2SO4 (180mL) and HNO3 (60mL). The solution was sonicated for two hours and heated under reflux at 80°C for 24h. The mixture was cooled and diluted with deionized (DI) water (800mL). The dark-brown GQDs solution was neutralized with Na2CO3. The final product solution was further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 1000Da) for 3days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: 1.613g of GQDs and excessive sodium borohydride were added into 20mL of tetrahydrofuran solution. Then the mixture was stirred gently for 8h at 70°C. The resulting mixture was subsequently vacuum-distilled to remove excessive sodium borohydride and then washed with ethanol several times, and finally the product solution was dialyzed as mentioned above.

The obtained rGQDs were firstly dissolved in 5.0mL of phosphate buffer (10.0mM, pH 7.4). Then pH of the rGQDs solution was adjusted to 5 to protonate carboxyl groups of rGQDs. After that, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimenthylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide (EDC) (80.0mg) and N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide sodium salt (Sulfo-NHS) (80.0mg) were added to the above solution to activate the surface carboxylic group for 30min at room temperature with continuous stirring. Subsequently, the activated rGQDs and cDNA (15.0μL, 100.0μM) were mixed together and allowed to react for 24h at room temperature with continuous stirring. The ssDNA–rGQDs bioconjugates were obtained by centrifugation and washing with PBS buffer three times to remove the free nonconjugated complex.

The quenching effect of CNTs on fluorescence of ssDNA–rGQDs was conducted as follows: 3.0mL of ssDNA–rGQDs suspension in buffer solution was prepared at first; then, a series of CNTs solutions with different concentrations were added into the resultant ssDNA–rGQDs solution which were finally diluted to 4.0mL with buffer solution; the fluorescence intensities of resulting mixtures were determined using fluorescence spectrometer after 5min of sonification and another 5min of standing. The detection of DNA was carried out as follows: 3.0mL of ssDNA–rGQDs suspension in buffer solution and 4.0µL of CNTs (1.75mgmL−1) were introduced into the resultant ssDNA–rGQDs solution at room temperature to quench the fluorescence. After 10min of mixing, different concentrations of complementary or mismatched DNA sequences targets were added into the above ssDNA–rGQDs solution, and the resulted solutions were finally diluted to 4.0mL. After 5min of sonification and 25min of standing at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity was measured under the optimal excitation wavelength. The fluorescence dependent on time of quenching and recovery were conducted by monitoring fluorescence intensity at different incubation times. The fluorescence intensity was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 285nm.

The morphologies of the products were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, which was performed on a JEOL-2100F instrument with accelerating voltage of 200KV. Samples were prepared by dropping ethanolic or aqueous suspensions of rGQDs, ssDNA-rGQDs probe and CNTs onto Cu TEM grids coated with a holey amorphous carbon film and following solvent evaporation in a dust protected atmosphere. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analyses were conducted using a Kratos Axis ULTRA X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with a 165nm hemispherical electron energy analyzer. The incident radiation came from monochromatic Al X-ray (1486.6eV) at 15kV and 3mA. Wide survey scans were taken at an analyzer pass energy of 160eV over a 1400–0eV binding energy with 1.0eV step and a dwell time of 100ms, while narrow multiplex higher resolution scans were performed at a pass energy of 20eV with 0.05eV step and a dwell time of 200ms. The pressure in analysis chamber was less than 7.5×10−9 Torr during sample analysis. The UV–vis spectra were carried out on a Perkin Elmer Lambda 950 spectrometer, in which the products were dispersed in solvent after ultrasonication for 30min. The photoluminescence spectra were conducted on a PerkinElmer LS-45 fluorescence spectrometer, and lifetimes were determined using a FLS920 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Determination of the quantum yields of fluorescent substances was accomplished by comparison of the wavelength integrated intensity of these functionalized products to that of the standard quinine sulfate. The optical density was kept below 0.05 to avoid inner filter effects. The quantum yields of fluorescent substances were calculated using
Ф =
Ф
s
[ ( I
A
s
n
2
) / (
I
s
A
n
s
2
) ]
where Ф is the quantum yield, I is the integrated intensity, A is the optical density and n is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript s refers to the standard reference of known quantum yield. Quinine sulfate was chosen as the standard, whose quantum yield is 0.577 and nearly constant for excitation wavelength from 200nm to 400nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes based nanosensor for DNA detection
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots possess good biocompatibility, strong fluorescence and high efficiency of FRET, and thus were chosen as the fluorophore in this DNA nanosensor. Multi-walled carbon nanotubes with unique one-dimensional structure and good dispersion in water after moderate oxidation were used as electron acceptor and quenching agent in FRET. Scheme 1 displays the schematic illustration of DNA detection strategy based on FRET between rGQDs labeled DNA probe and CNTs. To improve the fluorescence of probe and sensitivity of the as-prepared nanosensor, GQDs were reduced with NaBH4 to prepare rGQDs with high emission efficiency. Subsequent detection of target DNA is accomplished by the following three steps: in the first step, single-stranded DNA probe (ssDNA–rGQDs) is synthesized using connecting DNA and rGQDs through condensation reaction with EDC. The as-prepared probe ssDNA–rGQDs exhibits strong blue fluorescence due to the high quantum yield of rGQDs. In the second step, multi-walled carbon nanotubes are oxidized with concentrated nitric and sulfuric acid firstly to obtain oxidized carbon nanotubes (CNTs) with good solubility in water. Then, ssDNA–rGQDs probe is mixed with CNTs so that ssDNA–rGQDs can adsorb on the surface of CNTs through electrostatic attraction and π–π stacking interaction, where the assembly ssDNA–rGQDs/CNTs forms. The formation of assembly ssDNA–rGQDs/CNTs results in nearly complete quenching of fluorescence of ssDNA–rGQDs probe through FRET between them. The self-assembled ssDNA–rGQDs/CNTs can act as the nanosensor for target DNA (tDNA) detection. In the third step, upon the addition of tDNA, tDNA can capture ssDNA–rGQDs in the assembly ssDNA–rGQDs/CNTs to produce the dsDNA–rGQDs through specific base pairing. The formation of dsDNA–rGQDs breaks up electrostatic attraction and π–π stacking interaction between ssDNA–rGQDs and CNTs, and thus leads to liberation and detachment of dsDNA–rGQDs from CNTs, resulting in fluorescence recovery from free dsDNA–rGQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and construction of graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes based nanosensor for DNA detection
ce:section.ce:para: The pristine GQDs were prepared from graphite powder through oxidation with concentrated nitric and sulfuric acids and further purification. However, the pristine GQDs have very weak fluorescence. To substantially improve their fluorescence, the as-prepared GQDs were reduced by sodium borohydride to obtain reduced GQDs (rGQDs) with high emission efficiency. Both GQDs and rGQDs were dialyzed to remove the extra original compounds and produced salts with dialysis bag. The morphologies of GQDs and rGQDs are characterized by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) shown in Fig. S1A and B. They have similar morphology and size distribution. Fig. S1D shows the size of rGQDs span from 2 to 5nm, and mainly concentrate at 3 and 4nm. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image in Fig. S1C depicted the clear graphene-like crystalline structure of rGQDs, and the spacing of adjacent lattice planes is about 3.0±0.2Å, which is close to data of GQDs reported in reference and consistent with (002) diffraction plane of graphite (Li et al., 2010; Qian et al., 2013). The XPS spectra in Fig. S2A and B show both GQDs and rGQDs are mainly composed of carbon (at ca. 284.8eV) and oxygen (at ca. 531.4eV). Except for carbon and oxygen, some sodium from remaining sodium salts in treatment was also observed in XPS spectra. The harsh treatment of strong oxidants broke down graphite into nanosized dots with oxygen-rich surface groups including hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl and epoxy. This is verified by high content of oxygen (63.52%) for GQDs in Table S1 and XPS C1s peaks from C=O (288.6eV) and C–O (286.6eV) in Fig. S2C (Moulder et al., 1992; Zheng et al., 2011). Since the reduction with NaBH4 can selectively reduce the carbonyl and epoxy moieties to hydroxyl groups and retain other functional groups including C=C and COOH, rGQDs possess lower oxygen content by 2.73% in general (Table S1) and larger ratio of C–O to C=O groups than GQDs which is verified by the obvious enhancement of C–O group and substantial decrease of C=O group in intensity for rGQDs relative to those for GQDs shown in Fig. S2C and D. The comparison between GQDs and rGQDs in FT-IR shown in Fig. S3 demonstrated apparent increase of vibrational absorption peaks at 1140cm−1 from C–O and at 3437cm−1 from O–H, which confirmed the reduction of GQDs with NaBH4.

Multi-walled carbon nanotubes with less than 8nm diameter were moderately oxidized with concentrated nitric and sulfuric acids to obtain short carbon nanotubes with good solubility in water. The morphology of oxidized carbon nanotubes was characterized by TEM shown in Fig. 1A. The carbon nanotubes were substantially shortened into hundreds of nanometers after oxidation, but their tube-like structure was remained and their diameters of less than 8 in nanometer are close to those before oxidative treatment. Their rough surface and stable suspension in water indicated that oxidation introduced a great deal of oxygen-rich groups into surface of carbon nanotubes. For clarity, the as-prepared oxidized carbon nanotubes were denoted as CNTs. Good dispersion offers the key factor for CNTs to act as quenching agent. Thus, the next step is the preparation of rGQD-labeled DNA probe. The connecting DNA (cDNA) was modified with amino group for the subsequent connection with rGQDs. The condensation reaction was conducted between –COOH group of rGQDs and –NH2 group of cDNA with EDC reagent under the catalysis of Sulfo-NHS. TEM image in Fig. 1B showed their similar size and shape with those of rGQDs.

Fig. 2 shows the fluorescence spectra and time-resolved decay curves of all fluorescent materials involved in the detection route. The pristine GQDs present weak blue fluorescence with the central emission peak of 432nm, and their quantum yield is only 0.017 as shown in Table S2. However, after reduction with NaBH4, rGQDs exhibit strong blue light with central emission band at 443nm, and their emission efficiency is largely elevated to 0.204, which enables the strong fluorescence of the following fluorescent probe. The emission peak of ssDNA–rGQDs probe is red-shifted to 480nm relative to that of rGQDs, and thus ssDNA–rGQDs probe presents strong green fluorescence. Its high quantum yield of 0.21 contributes to the high sensitivity of the constructed nanosensor for DNA detection. The apparent differences in emission peak, lifetime and time-resolved decay curve among ssDNA–rGQDs, cDNA and rGQDs provide solid evidence for the successful preparation of ssDNA–rGQDs probe. Both cDNA and tDNA have very weak fluorescence with quantum yield of less than 0.017, implying that the strong fluorescence of probe and recovered fluorescence are not originated from them. The central emission peak of cDNA is 426nm, while the emission band of tDNA is at 410nm.

Their large difference in emission peak with probe fluorescence and recovered fluorescence further excludes the possibility of originality from cDNA or tDNA. The final fluorescent substance dsDNA–rGQDs formed between ssDNA–rGQDs and tDNA through specific base pairing possesses strong fluorescence with central emission of 485nm, which is apparently different with that of ssDNA–rGQDs or tDNA. From Table S2, one can notice that optimum excitation wavelengths, emission wavelengths and quantum yields between ssDNA–rGQDs and dsDNA–rGQDs are also substantially different. This revealed that the recovered fluorescence is from dsDNA–rGQDs and proved the feasibility of proposed route shown in Scheme 1. The formation of dsDNA–rGQDs is further verified by the substantial gaps in lifetime among ssDNA–rGQDs, dsDNA–rGQDs and tDNA and distinct time-resolved decay curve from the others since different fluorescent substances have distinct lifetimes and time-resolved decay curves. The quantum yield of dsDNA–rGQDs is up to 0.184, ensuring the good sensitivity for detection of tDNA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DNA with graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes based nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3A demonstrates the strong quenching effect of oxidized carbon nanotubes on the fluorescence of ssDNA–rGQDs and subsequent fluorescence recovery by tDNA, mDNA-1, mDNA-2 and mDNA-3. The fluorescence of ssDNA–rGQDs probe can be scarcely influenced by the addition of tDNA in the absence of CNTs, however, it can be largely quenched by CNTs upon addition of 3.2μgmL−1 of CNTs. The fluorescence intensity is decreased to only 20% of the original owing to the formation of non-fluorescent ssDNA–rGQDs/CNTs assembly. In addition, CNTs itself exhibits no fluorescence emission, which make no contribution to whole fluorescence intensity of each sample measured. TEM images in Fig. 1C and D display that ssDNA–rGQDs probes were adsorbed on the surface of CNTs, verifying the self-assembly between ssDNA–rGQDs and CNTs. Fig. 3B shows the UV–vis spectra of ssDNA–rGQDs, CNTs and their assembly ssDNA–rGQDs/CNTs. One can note that the probe ssDNA–rGQDs has two obvious absorbance peaks at around 230nm and 280nm. However, after addition of CNTs, the assembly ssDNA–rGQDs/CNTs still possesses two similar UV–vis absorbance peaks but cannot exhibit strong fluorescence relative to ssDNA–rGQDs, clearly indicating that fluorescence resonance energy transfer between ssDNA–rGQDs and CNTs in this assembly occurred. The quenched fluorescence can be efficiently recovered to 85% of the original intensity by addition of tDNA. Although mDNA-1 with one mismatched base can also lead to fluorescence recovery to 50% of the original intensity, its fluorescence recovery efficiency is much lower than that of tDNA. The addition of mDNA-2 with two mismatched bases and mDNA-3 with three mismatched bases only results in very weak fluorescence recovery, indicating the good selectivity of this method for detection of tDNA.

This specific fluorescence “on-off-on” process based on FRET between rGQDs and CNTs can serve as a sensitive nanosensor for quantitative DNA analysis. In order to develop this analytical method, optimal conditions including concentration of added CNTs, incubation time of fluorescence quenching and recovery were explored. Fig. 4A shows the quenching effect with various concentrations of CNTs, and the fluorescence gradually decreases with the increase of concentration of CNTs. When the concentration of CNTs is up to 3.2µgmL−1, the fluorescence intensity remains nearly unchanged. Thus, 3.2µgmL−1 was chosen as the optimal concentration of CNTs. The quenching effect with CNTs and recovery efficiency with tDNA as a function of incubation time are displayed in Fig. 4B. One can note that the fluorescence is gradually quenched with time, and remains nearly constant after 30 minutes. The curve for fluorescence recovery by tDNA shows a rapid growth in fluorescence intensity in the first 20min, and then keeps nearly unchanged in intensity after 30min. Thus, both the optimal fluorescence quenching time and recovery time were chosen as 30min in the following determination of tDNA.

Under the optimal conditions, the sensing performance of this nanosensor was evaluated by adding varying concentrations of tDNA into the proceeding ssDNA–rGQDs/CNTs solution. The fluorescence comes from dsDNA–rGQDs formed between ssDNA–rGQDs and tDNA after addition of tDNA into ssDNA–rGQDs/CNTs system. As illustrated in Fig. 5A, the fluorescence intensity of dsDNA–rGQDs is increased gradually with addition of tDNA because more and more amounts of dsDNA–rGQDs formed and escaped from CNTs surface during this process. The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and concentration of tDNA shown in Fig. 5B was established, and can be expressed as y=3.32x+109.39, where R 2=0.998. The established method for DNA detection has a broad linear range of 1.5–133.0 nM with a detection limit of 0.4nM as estimated from the derived calibration curve (>3 standard deviations). This is the first time to achieve sensitive analysis of DNA using nanosensor based on FRET between graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes. It is better than combined graphene and graphene quantum dots-based methods for detection of human immunoglobulin G previously reported by Zhao et al. (2013). It has greater advantage in biocompatibility than semiconductor quantum dots and carbon nanotubes based method for DNA detection reported by Zhang et al. (2013).

In summary, a novel and effective nanosensor based on FRET through regulating the interaction between graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes for detection of DNA has been established. It can serve as a universal strategy for DNA detection. The prepared DNA probe has similar superiorities in strong fluorescence, good biocompatibility and resistance to photobleaching with graphene quantum dots since graphene quantum dots serve as the fluorescent labeling function. Fluorescence resonance energy transfer can be easily achieved because of efficient self-assembly between graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes. This nanosensor can distinguish complementary and single-base-mismatched nucleic acid sequences with high sensitivity and good reproducibility. It shows a quite broad linear scope and ultralow detection limit for DNA detection, and thus possesses excellent performance in trace analysis. Since all the materials involved in the sensing system are of excellent biocompatibility, it is expected that this DNA detection method would be used in vivo and in vitro, and promotes the application of carbon-based nanomaterials in immunoassays.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21005073, 21275131, 21275130 and 21175118) and Zhejiang Province (Nos. LY13B050001, LQ13B050002 and 2011C3751).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2014.04.006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Handbook of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy – a reference book of standard spectra for identification and interpretation of XPS data
